analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 5 finding(s) from 9 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Potentially high-privilege reads are allowed without full debug authentication, but debug master's bus mastering is disabled, limiting exposure.
findings.vulnerability_category: read / fuse_mem internal hash data / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU bus master Operation: read Object: fuse_mem internal hash data Expected guard: jtag_unlock and proper privilege Observed behavior: fuse read path exists through pkt_wrapper, gated by acc_ctrl_c and possibly jtag_unlock. debug_master is disabled, but normal CPU may access. No explicit debug/test bypass evident. Path: CPU -> AXI -> noc_axilite_bridge -> pkt_wrapper -> fuse_req/addr/rdata -> fuse_mem Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If not properly restricted, machine-mode can read fuse hash; debug mode cannot bypass because debug master is deactivated.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full analysis requires pkt_wrapper internals to confirm whether jtag_unlock is strictly enforced and if debug_mode is used.

findings.summary: Debug mode likely overrides reglk_ctrl, creating a privilege escalation path.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / Peripheral configuration registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Debug master or CPU in debug/test mode Operation: write Object: Peripheral configuration registers Expected guard: reglk_ctrl lock bit must be checked; debug/test must not override. Observed behavior: Multiple peripheral wrappers (aes0, aes1, aes2, sha256, hmac, rsa) receive debug_mode_i. This suggests the wrappers may bypass reglk_ctrl when debug_mode_i is active. Path: debug_mode_i input -> i_aes0_wrapper.debug_mode_i, i_aes2_wrapper.debug_mode_i, i_hmac_wrapper.debug_mode_i, etc. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An attacker in debug mode could write to locked peripheral registers, overriding security protections.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Exact logic inside wrappers is unknown; need to inspect wrapper modules to confirm bypass.

findings.summary: High risk that debug mode can force bypass, breaking authentication.
findings.vulnerability_category: set key_hash_bypass_i / HMAC authentication strength / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master that can write to control register Operation: set key_hash_bypass_i Object: HMAC authentication strength Expected guard: Signal must be non-programmable or only by machine-mode with lock. Observed behavior: key_hash_bypass_i is an input to hmac module. In riscv_peripherals.sv, no direct connection found; it is likely inside hmac_wrapper. The wrapper receives debug_mode_i, which could be used to force the bypass. Path: hmac_wrapper.debug_mode_i -> internal logic -> key_hash_bypass_i Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: HMAC authentication can be completely bypassed, allowing JTAG unlock without correct hash.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need to inspect hmac_wrapper to see whether debug_mode_i drives key_hash_bypass_i.

findings.summary: Potential backdoor if we_flag sources are insecure.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / we_flag_* signals / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU or debug master Operation: write Object: we_flag_* signals Expected guard: Must require machine-mode and be locked; debug/test must not alter. Observed behavior: we_flag_* are inputs to riscv_peripherals, used to override locks (e.g., reglk_ctrl OR we_flag_1). The driving source is not visible; could be an MMIO register or external pin. Path: External driver -> we_flag_0..4 inputs Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: If any untrusted agent can assert we_flag, it can unlock protected peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Origin of we_flag signals; are they writeable by software?

findings.summary: Partial reset may leave authentication state intact.
findings.vulnerability_category: reset / JTAG authentication state (jtag_unlock_o, pass_check) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: reset controller / debug logic Operation: reset Object: JTAG authentication state (jtag_unlock_o, pass_check) Expected guard: All resets must clear jtag_unlock_o. Observed behavior: trst_ni resets flops but not pass_check combinationally; test_logic_reset only resets dr_q, not state_q. pass_check likely derived from state_q, so may remain set after test_logic_reset if state_q unchanged. Path: test_logic_reset -> dr_d; state_q is not reset by this signal. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: JTAG unlock could persist after a debug reset, allowing continued unauthorized access.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need to confirm assignment of jtag_unlock_o (likely pass_check) and verify state_q reset domains.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
